title: Violating the Dead and Oil Money
body:
Modern Chinese medical research and education, on the other hand, was an American import, paid for in Standard Oil money. China was a deliberate choice, reflecting the Rockefeller family’s longstanding ties to American Baptist missionary networks already running medical work there and the Foundation’s broader chartered mission to promote “the well-being of mankind throughout the world.”[[cite:rockarch-foundation-history]] 

The Rockefeller Foundation’s China Medical Board had been established in 1914 with a $12 million endowment, modeled on the Johns Hopkins approach that had recently reformed American medical education--research-university affiliation, full-time clinical faculty, and two years of lab-based basic-science training before clinical work, a stricter standard than the missionary teaching hospitals and apprenticeship traditions then common in China.[[cite:rockarch-pumc-history]] Its adviser Simon Flexner called the project that became PUMC “the Johns Hopkins of China.” In 1915, the Board purchased the Union Medical College from the London Missionary Society and rebuilt it on the site of a former royal palace in Beijing. 

Formally established in 1917, Peking Union Medical College was conceived as nothing less than “a medical school and hospital comparable with that of the leading institutions of Western civilization.”[[cite:rockarch-pumc-history]] Its small graduating classes, capped at thirty to fifty students, produced an outsized share of the country’s first generation of Western-trained medical scientists. The Harvard-trained biochemist Hsien Wu (Wu Xian) became the first Chinese to head a PUMC department, organizing its biochemistry section in 1924, and is generally credited as the founder of Chinese biochemistry as a research discipline.[[cite:zheng-hsien-wu-2012]][[cite:pmc-cams-pumc-2020]]

The Republican-era model didn’t survive the war or the political transition that followed it. Japanese forces seized PUMC in January 1942, shortly after Pearl Harbor, and shut it down for the duration of the war; medical research elsewhere scattered as scientists fled inland with whatever equipment they could carry. In January 1951, the new People’s Republic nationalized the institution, ending the era of American-funded Chinese medical education.[[cite:pumc-wikipedia]]

title: Great Leaps Forward
body:
Mao did not trust science, but he trusted the Russians. When the People's Republic was founded in 1949, the new [[entity:cas|Chinese Academy of Sciences]], was modeled on its Soviet counterpart, inheriting their biology alongside their institutional scaffolding. The most damaging import was the theories of Trofim Lysenko, who had won Stalin’s favor in the 1930s by claiming that acquired traits could be inherited[[note:aka Lamarckism]] and that Mendelian genetics was a bourgeois fraud. 

Alternative genetic theories were permitted only after Khrushchev’s “Secret Speech” of February 1956, a closed-door denunciation of Stalin at the 20th Party Congress in Moscow. Chinese geneticists led by Tan Jiazhen, a Caltech-trained Drosophila researcher, used the Qingdao Genetics Symposium that same year to argue publicly for Mendelian inheritance--the first time anyone had done so since the founding of the People’s Republic. 

However, Lysenkoism also extended, far more fatally, into the Chinese agricultural system. In 1958, Mao issued the “Eight-Point Charter of Agriculture,” directly influenced by his doctrines of close-planting and deep ploughing. Such practices contributed to the catastrophic crop failures of the Great Leap Forward and, by extension, the deadliest famine in human history. Estimates range from 15 to 45 million deaths.[[cite:cas-history-soviet-model]][[cite:lysenkoism-wikipedia]]

Miraculously, two major scientific projects still emerged from this period. The first was the total chemical synthesis of bovine insulin. Deciphered by Frederick Sanger, it was the first full protein sequence ever determined, and synthesis was the obvious next frontier. Wang Yinglai began the project in 1958 and saw through its conclusion in 17 September 1965, 8 months before the start of the Cultural Revolution. The accomplishment was nominated for the Nobel multiple times, but Communist policy only recognized collective effort, and the Chinese Academy of Sciences refused to name the contributing scientists. Wang spent the next decade under house arrest in his own institute, attending struggle sessions on Mao Zedong Thought.[[cite:bcas-insulin-2024]][[cite:synthetic-insulin-wikipedia]]

The second was artemisinin. On 23 May 1967, the People’s Liberation Army launched Project 523, a top-secret military effort to find a treatment for the chloroquine-resistant malaria killing Chinese and Vietnamese troops in the jungles of Southeast Asia. The military framing put the project under the protection of the People's Liberation Army, exempting it from the Cultural Revolution’s wider attack on academic science. Tu Youyou, a 38-year-old researcher at the [[entity:cacms|Academy of Traditional Chinese Medicine]] trained in both modern pharmacy and classical Chinese pharmacology, was appointed to lead the herbal-medicine team in January 1969. Naturally, her group started by screening candidates from classical Chinese texts and folk medicine, where they found qinghao (青蒿, Artemisia annua, “sweet wormwood”), prescribed in Chinese medicine for intermittent fevers for some 1,500 years. The drug was inconsistently effective against malaria parasites, and Tu ended up identifying the reason why in a 4th-century manuscript--Ge Hong’s Zhouhou Beijifang prescribed soaking 青蒿 in cold water rather than boiling it, from which Tu inferred that her standard heated ethanol-reflux extraction was destroying the drug's active compound. She switched to diethyl ether, which boils at 35°C, and the new extract produced complete inhibition in mouse and monkey models. 

In the absence of clinical trial procedures, Tu and two colleagues dosed themselves to check for toxicity. And seeing no ill effects, they went onto deployed the medicine to 21 more patients in Hainan. The active compound, qinghaosu (青蒿素), earned Tu the Lasker Award in 2011 and the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2015.[[cite:tu-youyou-wikipedia]]

Such successes were exceptions in a half-century of state-led attacks on Chinese science. The Cultural Revolution (1966–1976) closed universities to new undergraduates between 1966 and 1969 and to graduate students through 1977; the academy’s budget was slashed to less than one-sixth of its 1965 level by 1967; records document 229 scientists killed or driven to suicide.

title: Rebuilding Science After Mao
shift: Deng-era state reinvestment in science via the [[reform:863-program|863 Program]] (1986), the [[reform:973-program|973 Basic Research Program]] (1997), and recruitment of [[reform:thousand-talents|haigui returnees]], with founders bootstrapping companies by manufacturing discarded off-patent generics.
outcomes: [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui Medicine]] founded 1990 by Sun Piaoyang pivoting a state pharma factory in Lianyungang into oncology research.
[[entity:hansoh|Hansoh Pharmaceutical]] founded 1995 by Sun's wife Zhong Huijuan; both bootstrapped via off-patent generics.

[[entity:3sbio|3SBio]] founded 1993; launched China's first domestically manufactured EPO (EPIAO) in 1998, the first Chinese-produced recombinant protein on the market. [[metric:recombinant:2|recombinant rung 2]]
[[reform:863-program|863 Program]] funded 5,200+ R&D projects before consolidation in 2016, paired with [[reform:973-program|973]] to build domestic lab capacity and basic-science bench.

[[reform:thousand-talents|Haigui program]] began early 1990s, drawing overseas-trained Chinese scientists with lab budgets and faculty posts; ~7,000 returnees by 2017 anchored BeiGene, Innovent, and Hengrui science teams.

inlineCharts.title: NSFC annual budget, 1986–2000
inlineCharts.caption: Approximate, CNY millions. Founded 1986 as China's first peer-reviewed competitive grants agency, the National Natural Science Foundation built the basic-science infrastructure that, alongside the 863 (1986) and 973 (1997) Programs, anchored Deng-era reinvestment in scientific research.

body:
Mao died in September 1976. In March 1978, with Deng Xiaoping ascendant, the National Science Conference declared the rehabilitation of science and technology a strategic priority.[[cite:national-science-conference-1978]]

[[chart:nsfc-budget-buildup]]

Among the companies that emerged in this period was [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui Pharmaceutical]], currently the largest Chinese pharma company by market cap. The story behind the company has become somewhat of a Chinese myth and a classic archetype of the time period. Yicai starts their biography like so:

Similar to many entrepreneurs, the story of Sun Piaoyang and Hengrui Pharmaceutical began in a small state-owned factory.

In 1982, Sun Piaoyang graduated from China Pharmaceutical University and joined Lianyungang Pharmaceutical Factory as a technician.[[cite:yicai-hengrui-sun-piaoyang-2024]] He became factory director in 1990. The compounds the factory produced (spiramycin and erythromycin) were "sold for a penny" with no path to profitability without developing new drugs of their own.

He launched the anti-cancer injection VP16 project in 1991, an almost untouched market in China at the time. The factory's profit jumped from ~¥80,000 to over a million.

The following year, in 1992, Sun spent ~¥1.2 million, almost all of the company's profits, on a license to make and sell ifosfamide via a new Chinese-developed synthesis route from the Institute of Pharmaceutical Research of the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences. He was 34 and newly married, but in his words, "without technology, your fate is in others' hands." It was miraculous that his risks paid off--the factory's revenue exceeded ¥100 million in 1996.[[cite:yicai-hengrui-sun-piaoyang-2024]]

It can be said that today's Hengrui Medicine is inseparable from Sun Piaoyang's courage to break the game.

Sun was certainly not a one-trick pony. In 1995 he founded a second pharma company, [[entity:hansoh|Hansoh Pharmaceutical]], alongside a Hong Kong investor, Cen Junda. Too busy with Hengrui to run the new firm, Sun handed it to his wife Zhong Huijuan, a chemistry teacher at a middle school five minutes by bike from the Lianyungang factory.[[cite:scmp-zhong-huijuan-hansoh-ipo]][[cite:fiercepharma-hansoh-hengrui-family]] Both companies have become wildly successful, with the couple's combined net worth at roughly $34.7 billion in 2026.[[cite:hurun-china-rich-list-2025-hengrui-hansoh]] Despite their now global reach, they continue to live in Lianyungang, the town where they first met.

title: Human Genome Project, Chinese CROs, and Science Parks
shift: Haigui that returned to China brought platform technologies (genomics sequencing, combinatorial chemistry, CRO models) and combined them with state park subsidies, cost arbitrage, and [[reform:863-program|863 Program]] funding to build China's first physical-and-institutional R&D infrastructure, anchored by [[entity:bgi|BGI]] and [[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi PharmaTech]].
outcomes: [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] founded 9 September 1999; secured China's seat in the international Human Genome Project consortium via the 1% Project (sequencing chromosome 3 short arm).
[[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi PharmaTech]] established in Shanghai's Waigaoqiao Free Trade Zone as China's first domestic CRO, scaling combinatorial chemistry at a fraction of US labor costs.

Shanghai's Zhangjiang High-Tech Park attracted [[entity:roche|Roche]], [[entity:astrazeneca|AstraZeneca]], [[entity:novartis|Novartis]], and [[entity:gsk|GSK]] by the late 2000s; MNC-trained Chinese scientists cycled out to seed local startups within 3–5 years.
BioBAY Suzhou opened June 2007 as a startup-focused complement to Zhangjiang.
[[entity:remegen|RemeGen]] founded 2008 in Yantai to develop antibody-drug conjugates, putting the first Chinese ADC platform in place. [[metric:adc:1|ADC rung 1]]
body:
The Human Genome Project officially began in 1990, an international effort led by the US National Institutes of Health and Department of Energy alongside the UK and France, with Japan and Germany joining later. Chinese genomics infrastructure was sparse at the time. The National Human Genome Center was the only domestic institution focused on the field, and there was no real push to join the international effort until 1997, when a group of four academics newly returned from overseas as the first wave of haigui convinced the [[entity:cas|Chinese Academy of Sciences]] to establish a Human Genome Research Center.[[cite:chgc-hgp-china-protein-cell]][[cite:bgi-history-genomics-cn]]

The new institution compiled a proposal to sequence ~30 megabases on the short arm of chromosome 3. Referred to as the "Beijing Region," it was believed to be linked to esophageal cancer, a disease with extraordinarily high regional incidence in Linxian (Henan) and parts of Sichuan.[[cite:chgc-hgp-china-protein-cell]] On September 1, 1999, the proposal was officially approved by the International Genome Sequencing Consortium, making China the only developing country in the project.[[cite:big-20th-anniversary-2024]]

Eight days later, on September 9, 1999 at 9:09:09[[note:A deliberate numerological flourish: 九 (jiǔ, "nine") sounds like 久 (jiǔ, "long-lasting").]], Yang Huanming, Wang Jian, Yu Jun, and Liu Siqi formally established [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]].[[cite:bgi-history-genomics-cn]] Wang Jian would later say,

这个时间不是我们刻意挑选的，它是天意。
We didn't deliberately pick this time. It was heaven's will.

Between October 1999 and March 2000, China had to complete 500,000 Sanger sequencing reactions, an effort distributed across 15 teams of the Chinese Human Genome Consortium and centered at BGI's converted building in Beijing's Shunyi District, where ABI 3700 capillary sequencers ran in 24-hour shifts.[[cite:chgc-hgp-china-protein-cell]]

title: Hybridizing with America and Fast-Following Keytruda
shift: Overseas-trained returnees recruited under the [[reform:thousand-talents|Thousand Talents Program]] paired with in-licensing of Western platforms and Nasdaq/Hong Kong capital to build hybrid US-China biotech firms that could run Phase III at Chinese cost.
outcomes: Annual returnees from overseas study quintupled from 69,300 (2008) to 364,800 (2014), filling founding teams at [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] (2010), [[entity:innovent|Innovent]] (2011), and [[entity:junshi|Junshi]] (2012). [[metric:novelMab:1|mAb rung 1]]

[[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] co-founder Xiaodong Wang leveraged his HHMI credential and John Oyler's US VC network to raise venture capital early, then ran FDA-grade Phase III trials inside China at one-third the US cost; IPO'd Nasdaq February 2016.

[[entity:innovent|Innovent]] licensed Western antibody platforms (2011) and raised Series A from Boston VCs; [[entity:junshi|Junshi]] focused a domestic PD-1 program on an unvalidated Western target (2012); [[entity:zailab|Zai Lab]] ran a pure in-licensing portfolio (2014).

By chapter close, BeiGene, Innovent, Junshi, and Hengrui were running Chinese PD-1/L1 programs copying Merck Keytruda's validated mechanism; the pipeline would reach ~85 candidates and the world's most prolific Keytruda combination-trial source by 2020.

[[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]] proved the fast-follower small-molecule model viable, with apatinib (Oct 2014) becoming the first Chinese-developed angiogenesis inhibitor on the NMPA market; later additions: pyrotinib 2018, camrelizumab 2019. [[metric:smallMol:2|small mol rung 2]] [[metric:outLicensingDealValueBn|out-licensing $]]

inlineCharts.title: Annual returnees from overseas study
inlineCharts.caption: Thousand Talents Program launched late 2008. Annual returnee flow quintupled over the 2008-2014 founding window for BeiGene, Innovent, and Junshi. Series compiled from MoE annual press conferences; intermediate years are MoE-published estimates.

body:
[[chart:returnees-per-year]]

[[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] was the first company built on the hybrid model, co-founded in 2010 by the American entrepreneur John Oyler and Xiaodong Wang, a Howard Hughes Medical Institute investigator who had left UT Southwestern in 2003 to found and direct [[entity:nibs-beijing|NIBS Beijing]]. Wang's HHMI credential gave the company scientific legitimacy, Oyler's American network let BeiGene raise from US venture firms early, and the company built FDA-grade clinical operations inside China to run global trials at Chinese cost.[[cite:beigene-wikipedia]]

title: Harmonization and validation
shift: [[reform:order-117|Order 117's]] 2015 fraud purge, [[reform:priority-review|priority review]], [[reform:ich-join|ICH membership]], and [[reform:mah|MAH licensing]] together brought NMPA reviews into equivalence with FDA and EMA standards, redirecting the agency from a fraud-clogged backlog to a globally credible pipeline.
outcomes: [[reform:order-117|Order 117 self-inspection]] purged 1,165 of 1,622 pending applications with new clinical data; alongside a CDE reviewer ramp-up (~70 in 2015 to >800 by 2018) and [[reform:priority-review|priority review]], the broader NDA backlog fell from ~22,000 in Sept 2015 to under 3,500 by end-2018.

CTA approval timelines fell from ~12 months to 60 working days under [[reform:priority-review|priority review]]; after [[reform:ich-join|ICH membership]] (June 2017), foreign trial data became admissible.
[[entity:beigene|BeiGene's]] Brukinsa cleared [[entity:fda|FDA]] November 2019, the first China-developed cancer drug approved in the US. [[metric:smallMol:5|small mol rung 5]]

[[entity:henlius|Henlius's]] Hanlikang biosimilar (rituximab) cleared NMPA February 2019, the first Chinese-made biosimilar; homegrown checkpoint inhibitors from [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]] and BeiGene followed the same year. [[metric:biosimilar:2|biosimilar rung 2]] [[metric:novelMab:4|mAb rung 4]]

[[entity:legend|Legend Biotech]] entered a worldwide BCMA CAR-T partnership with Janssen ([[entity:johnson-johnson|J&J]]) for cilta-cel in December 2017, the first Western-pharma deal for a Chinese-discovered cell therapy. [[metric:cellTherapy:4|cell therapy rung 4]]
[[entity:hansoh|Hansoh's]] peg-loxenatide cleared NMPA in 2018, the first Chinese-produced long-acting GLP-1 peptide. [[metric:peptide:2|peptide rung 2]]

inlineCharts.title: Pending NDAs at the NMPA
inlineCharts.caption: Order 117 (July 2015) directly purged 1,165 of 1,622 pending applications with new clinical data, but the broader 22k → 3.5k collapse came from a combined reform package: CDE reviewer hiring (~70 → >800 staff), priority review, and generic consistency evaluation. Year-end figures are approximate, anchored on the Sept-2015 peak (~22k) and the 2018 endpoint (under 3.5k).

body:
The Chinese State Drug Administration was established in 1998 under Zheng Xiaoyu (郑筱萸), reorganized in 2003 as the State Food and Drug Administration with Zheng continuing as director. Over his 7-year tenure (1998 through 2005), Zheng took roughly ¥6.49 million in bribes from eight pharmaceutical and medical-device companies in exchange for personally approving applications, including six drugs later found to be counterfeit. One of them, manufactured by Anhui Huayuan Worldbest Biology Pharmacy, killed 14 patients and left hundreds permanently disabled.[[cite:zheng-xiaoyu-wikipedia]] Zheng was executed by lethal injection on July 10, 2007, the fourth minister-level state official to face the death penalty in the post-Mao era. In subsequent years, the CFDA underwent significant reform but still struggled with a lack of manpower and proper administrative oversight.

When Bi Jingquan was appointed director of the [[entity:nmpa|CFDA]] in January 2015, there was a backlog of roughly 22,000 pending applications, mostly low-quality generic and "me-too" filings, with approval timelines stretching past a year. Bi was formerly vice-chair of the [[entity:ndrc|National Development and Reform Commission]], China's top macroeconomic planning body. He had no expertise in pharma but a track record as a well-oiled bureaucrat with significant political leverage.[[cite:bi-jingquan-wikipedia]]

When he issued [[reform:order-117|Order 117]] for companies to self-inspect their clinical trial data, nearly 80% of the applications were withdrawn. Bi expanded the technical review staff from roughly 120 to over 800,[[cite:petrieflom-china-drug-reform-2015]] and introduced [[reform:priority-review|priority review]] to fast-track innovative drugs over me-toos.

[[chart:nda-backlog-purge]]

He created the [[reform:mah|Marketing Authorization Holder (MAH)]] system, separating drug licenses from the authorization to manufacture them. Before MAH, a license was tied to a specific manufacturing facility, meaning research-heavy biotechs could not hold drug licenses without owning factories. MAH made asset-light biotechs like [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]], [[entity:innovent|Innovent]], and [[entity:junshi|Junshi]] structurally possible for the first time.

He entered China into the [[reform:ich-join|International Council for Harmonisation]], which made foreign clinical trial data admissible for Chinese registration and aligned Chinese standards with the FDA and EMA. Chinese biotechs could now run trials elsewhere and use the data to file in China, and foreign biotechs could market drugs in China without redoing trials in the Chinese system.

Despite Bi Jingquan's legendary track record and status as the architect of Chinese biotech reform, he was expelled from the Chinese Communist Party on December 9, 2025. The CCDI announcement said he had "accepted gifts, money, trips and banquets and borrowed vehicles from private business owners," "allowed others to pay for him," "secured benefits for others in recruitment," "sought profit for relatives against regulations," and "failed to maintain proper family conduct."[[cite:scmp-bi-jingquan-expelled-2025]] His central power had enabled great reform, and inevitably invited the temptations of wealth. It is rare that people with access to both levers do not eventually take the elucidated shortcuts.

title: Public capital for pre-revenue biotech
shift: [[reform:hk-18a|HKEX Chapter 18A]] (April 2018) and the [[reform:star-market|STAR Market]] (July 2019) opened the first public-capital channel for pre-revenue biotech outside the US, packaged with HKEX's broader move to allow weighted voting rights.
outcomes: Fewer than ten innovative Chinese biotechs were publicly traded anywhere in 2016; by late 2025, 75+ had listed via [[reform:hk-18a|Chapter 18A]] alone, raising over $30B and making [[entity:hkex|HKEX]] the world's second-largest biotech fundraising venue.
China-origin biotech market cap rose roughly 47-fold between 2016 and 2020 ($3B → $140B). [[metric:primaryMarketFinancingBn|primary-market $]]

[[entity:amgen|Amgen]] paid $2.7B for 20.5% of [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] plus a 20-asset co-development pact (October 2019), the first top-ten multinational equity bet, priced off the new Chinese public comparables. [[metric:outLicensingDealValueBn|out-licensing $]]

inlineCharts.title: Cumulative pre-revenue biotech listings, HKEX 18A vs STAR Market
inlineCharts.caption: HKEX Chapter 18A launched April 2018; STAR Market launched July 2019. The 75+ / $30B endpoint for 18A is documented; STAR series approximate.

body:
Charles Li Xiaojia (李小加) was born in Beijing in 1961. His teenage years coincided with the Cultural Revolution. He avoided being sent to the countryside under Mao's "up to the mountains, down to the countryside" campaign by taking a work assignment on an offshore oil rig in the Bohai Sea. When the universities reopened, he studied English literature at Xiamen University, graduating in 1984, and took his first professional job as an editor-reporter at China Daily, the Party's English-language newspaper. A scholarship, won on the back of a piece about corruption in Chinese soccer, took him to the United States, where he applied for schools in alphabetical order and took an offer at the University of Alabama. Columbia Law School followed in 1991, then fifteen years on Wall Street as the primary point of contact for a wave of Chinese SOE listings abroad, including China Mobile, PetroChina, Sinopec, and the big-four banks.[[cite:charles-li-wikipedia]]

In June 2009 he was named CEO of [[entity:hkex|Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing]], the first mainland-born executive to lead the institution. He took office in January 2010 and stayed eleven years.

The defining accomplishment was infrastructure. The Shanghai-Hong Kong Stock Connect in November 2014 created, for the first time, a regulated channel through which mainland Chinese investors could buy Hong Kong stocks and global investors could buy Shanghai-listed A-shares. Shenzhen-HK Connect followed in 2016, Bond Connect in 2017. The new capital channels turned Hong Kong into the world's number-one IPO venue in seven of his eleven years.

In 2013, Alibaba was preparing the largest IPO in history and wanted to list in Hong Kong. The structure they proposed was unusual. Alibaba's shares would be one-share-one-vote on paper, but board nominations would be controlled exclusively by the Alibaba Partnership, a 28-person internal group dominated by Jack Ma and Joe Tsai that could renominate its own candidates indefinitely if shareholders voted them down.

HKEX rules at the time prohibited it. Hong Kong had banned dual-class share structures in 1989 after two decades of family-controlled conglomerates abusing them at minority shareholders' expense, and the regulatory establishment, particularly the independent Securities and Futures Commission, held that one-share-one-vote was a foundational element of Hong Kong's investor-protection regime. Through 2013, HKEX and Alibaba tried to find a workaround.

On October 24, 2013, Li published a piece on the HKEX corporate blog titled "Taking the Debate Forward: Should Hong Kong Act on Non-Standard Shareholding Structures?" He framed the entire piece as a dream in which nine archetypal characters, Mr. Tradition, Mr. Innovation, Mr. Disclosure, Mr. Big Fund, Ms. Small Retail, Ms. Pragmatic, Mr. Morality, Ms. Future, and Mr. Procedure, visit him in his sleep and argue with each other about whether Hong Kong should accommodate new-economy companies with non-traditional governance. Li listens. He does not take sides.

Losing one or two listing candidates is not a big deal for Hong Kong; but losing a generation of companies from China's new economy is.

Li acknowledged the stakes clearly. He could not, as the sitting CEO of a public exchange, advocate for reversing a 24-year-old investor-protection rule on the strength of one IPO; the blog post illustrated the complexity of his position.

By month's end, Alibaba had given up on Hong Kong. On September 19, 2014, they listed on the NYSE. Priced at $68, opened at $92.70, raised $25 billion, the largest IPO in history. The secondary trading volume, which HKEX could have monetized, has run in the billions of dollars per day for the decade since. All of it went to New York.[[cite:scmp-alibaba-loss-hk-2014]]

Li spent the next four years rebuilding the political coalition for change, this time with the Alibaba precedent as evidence. The package finally landed in April 2018 as Chapter 8A, allowing weighted voting rights for innovative companies; [[reform:hk-18a|Chapter 18A]], allowing pre-revenue biotech listings; and Chapter 19C, allowing secondary listings for Greater China companies already public in the US or UK. Chapter 19C was specifically designed as the path for Alibaba to return to Hong Kong. In November 2019, five years and two months after the NYSE listing, Alibaba executed a secondary listing on HKEX, raising $11.2 billion.[[cite:charltons-chapter-18a]]

[[chart:hkex-18a-listings]]

In the same window as Bi Jingquan's CFDA reforms, [[reform:mah|MAH]] (2015) and [[reform:ich-join|ICH membership]] (June 2017), Chapter 18A gave Chinese biotechs a public-market venue. [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]]'s Hong Kong secondary listing in August 2018 was the proof of concept. By end-2021, 48 biotechs had listed via 18A, raising about US$14 billion;[[cite:law-asia-chapter-18a-end-2021]] by late 2025, more than 75 had raised over $30 billion. Regulatory reform in Beijing increased supply, and the listing reform in Hong Kong provided the venue for valuation.

title: Illegal CRISPR Babies
body:
China's debut on the global pharma stage not without scandal. In April 2015, Junjiu Huang at Sun Yat-sen University published the first report of CRISPR-mediated gene editing[[note:CRISPR-Cas9 uses a guide RNA to direct a DNA-cutting enzyme to a precise genomic site. The resulting double-strand break is repaired imperfectly, either knocking out the gene or pasting in new DNA.]] in human zygotes (non-viable tripronuclear embryos), an attempt to correct a β-globin mutation that causes thalassemia. The paper had been rejected by Nature and Science on ethical grounds before appearing in Protein & Cell,[[cite:liang-2015-crispr-tripronuclear]] and within weeks the International Summit on Human Gene Editing concluded that clinical use of germline editing would be “irresponsible.”[[cite:intl-summit-gene-editing-2015]] The following year, Chinese researchers conducted the first reported human trial using CRISPR-edited cells.

On 25–26 November 2018, on the eve of the Second International Summit on Human Genome Editing in Hong Kong, the Shenzhen-based biophysicist [[reform:he-jiankui|He Jiankui]] announced the birth of twin girls whose CCR5 gene, the immune-cell receptor that HIV uses to enter T cells, had been edited at the single-cell stage to confer HIV resistance. Not only was the editing technically sloppy (the twins were genetic mosaics with off-target effects), the ethical-review documents were discovered to have been forged.[[cite:wikipedia-he-jiankui-affair]]

The case closed in December 2019 with a three-year prison sentence for “illegal medical practice.”[[cite:statnews-he-jiankui-sentenced]] Beijing then pushed through a substantive legal overhaul: the [[reform:hgr-2019|2019 Regulations on Human Genetic Resources]], the 2020 Civil Code and [[reform:biosecurity-law|Biosafety Law]], and the 2020 eleventh amendment to the Criminal Law (which criminalized illegal human gene editing with up to seven years’ imprisonment).[[cite:covington-hgr-regulation]][[cite:covington-biosecurity-law]]

Since leaving prison, He Jiankui has been offered a position at a state-sponsored medical academy in Shenzhen. He remains occasionally active on X, where he tallies his funding (currently at $25,073 from 715 private donors) and invites his followers to nightclubs in Beijing. His current 10-year plan includes receiving the Nobel prize in 2029 and being canonized as a Roman Catholic saint in 2030.

title: Losing the COVID Race
body:
Then came COVID-19. By February 2020, parallel vaccine-development tracks ran across at least five technology platforms[[note:inactivated, recombinant subunit, adenovirus-vectored, attenuated influenza-vectored, and messenger-RNA]]. [[entity:sinovac|Sinovac’s]] [[entity:sinovac|CoronaVac]] and [[entity:sinopharm|Sinopharm’s]] [[entity:sinopharm|BBIBP-CorV]] would receive [[entity:who|World Health Organization]] Emergency Use Listing in 2021 and ship into dozens of low- and middle-income countries.[[cite:who-sinovac-eul]][[cite:who-sinopharm-eul]] 

The messenger-RNA story, however, was an embarrassment. China entered the pandemic without a mature lipid-nanoparticle delivery platform, and the LNP IP that Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna built on was held by Canadian pharmaceuticals that refused to licensed to Chinese sponsors. The [[entity:walvax|Walvax]]-[[entity:abogen|Abogen]]-Academy of Military Medical Sciences candidate [[entity:walvax|ARCoV]] reached Phase III in Mexico and Indonesia by mid-2021,[[cite:scmp-arcov-phase3-mexico-indonesia]] but by that point [[entity:sinovac|Sinovac]] and [[entity:sinopharm|Sinopharm]] inactivated vaccines had already saturated domestic and Belt-and-Road demand, and [[entity:pfizer|Pfizer]]-[[entity:biontech|BioNTech]] and [[entity:moderna|Moderna]] had captured nearly every Western market. Beijing added mRNA to the 14th Five-Year Plan as a strategic-priority platform in 2021, after the race had effectively ended.

Even so, 2020 was a record-breaking year for Chinese biotech financing. HKEX Chapter 18A and the STAR Market had each been open for two years, the global zero-rate environment[[note:Major central banks cut policy rates to near-zero by March 2020 and ran aggressive asset-purchase programs in response to COVID. The resulting collapse in discount rates lifts long-duration valuations like clinical-stage biotech disproportionately, since most of the value sits in cash flows seven to fifteen years out. The XBI biotech ETF more than doubled from its March 2020 low through early 2021.]] was lifting every biotech valuation, and COVID had concentrated investor attention on the sector. The combined market cap of China-originated biotechs listed on Nasdaq, HKEX, and STAR rose 47-fold between 2016 and 2020[[note:From $3 billion in 2016 to $140 billion by year-end 2020.]].

title: Commoditization of the Fast-Followers
shift: [[reform:vbp|Volume-based procurement]] and the [[entity:cde|CDE]]'s [[reform:value-oriented-onc|clinical-value-oriented oncology guidelines]] (2021) eliminated competitive differentiation for me-too drugs, forcing margin collapse and mass licensing-out during a broader financing reset.
outcomes: Chinese primary-market financing collapsed from RMB 88B (2021 peak) to RMB 27.7B by 2024. [[metric:primaryMarketFinancingBn|primary-market $]]

[[entity:remegen|RemeGen]] licensed its first homegrown ADC to [[entity:seagen|Seagen]] for $2.6B in August 2021, a record for Chinese oncology and the first wholesale shift from internal development to Western partnership.

[[entity:merck-co|Merck]] expanded its ADC pact with [[entity:kelun-bio|Kelun Biotech]] to $9B in December 2022, signalling that Western pharma would take entire Chinese ADC pipelines rather than cherry-pick assets.
PD-1 price cuts averaged 50–70% under NRDL and [[reform:vbp|VBP]] tenders; over 80 PD-1 programs that had entered Phase III were defunded or shelved within 24 months of the 2021 guideline.
body:
The fast-follower wave had produced dozens of nearly-identical Chinese checkpoint inhibitors and ADCs[[note:An antibody-drug conjugate links a tumor-targeting antibody to a potent cytotoxin via a chemical linker. The antibody delivers the payload directly to cancer cells, sparing healthy tissue from chemotherapy-grade toxicity.]] competing for the same reimbursement slots, with otherwise lucrative therapeutics essentially evaporating in value once they hit the Chinese market.[[cite:pharmadj-cde-clinical-value-guidelines]]

This meant the only viable route for Chinese therapeutics producing ADCs and Merck fast-followers was to out-license to Western pharma. And luckily for them, there were plenty of buyers. In August 2021, [[entity:remegen|RemeGen]] became the first Chinese biotech to out-license its homegrown ADC to [[entity:seagen|Seagen]]. By December 2022, [[entity:merck-co|Merck]] was also signalling confidence in Chinese pipelines, expanding its ADC partnership with [[entity:kelun-bio|Kelun Biotech]], the oncology arm of Sichuan Kelun Pharmaceutical.[[cite:merck-kelun-adc-deal-press]]

title: Becoming a Global Supplier
shift: A decade of returnee-driven R&D buildup, [[reform:ich-join|ICH-aligned]] trials running at a fraction of US cost, and capability that matured past fast-following into novel constructs (cell therapy, bispecifics, ADCs) turned Chinese biotech into the cheapest path to a clinical-stage drug for Western pharma.
outcomes: Out-licensing of Chinese-discovered assets exploded from $16.6B (2023) to $41.5B (2024) to roughly $135.7B across 157 transactions in 2025; upfront payments now exceed primary-market IPO proceeds in China. [[metric:outLicensingDealValueBn|out-licensing $]]

[[entity:legend|Carvykti]] FDA-approved February 2022, the first China-discovered cell therapy on the US market and the first novel construct (dual-VHH BCMA) rather than a fast-follower. [[metric:cellTherapy:5|cell therapy rung 5]]

[[entity:akeso|Akeso]]'s ivonescimab beat [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]'s Keytruda monotherapy head-to-head in HARMONi-2 (Sept 2024), with a 49% lower risk of disease progression or death. [[metric:bispecific:5|bispecific rung 5]]

[[entity:hansoh|Hansoh]]–[[entity:gsk|GSK]] back-to-back $1.5B+ ADC deals (late 2023); Chinese assets supplied roughly 28% of all in-licensed innovator drugs at large pharma globally by 2024. [[metric:adc:4|adc rung 4]]

inlineCharts.title: CAR-T programs in development, China vs US
inlineCharts.caption: Programs at any clinical or preclinical stage. China overtook the US around 2020 and reached roughly 1.5 programs per US program by 2022. Series shape is approximate; the 2022 ratio is the documented anchor.

body:
[[chart:cart-inversion]]

By 2022, Chinese companies were running 1.5 cell-therapy programs for every one in American development, a complete inversion from a decade earlier when the US had developed or co-developed more than half of all candidates in the class.[[cite:pharma-tech-cart-china-superpower]] Chinese hospital ethics committees were clearing investigator-initiated CAR-T trials[[note:An investigator-initiated trial is a clinical study designed and run by an academic or hospital researcher rather than a pharma sponsor. In China, hospital-level ethics committees retain broad authority to approve and run them on their own timelines, often months rather than the year-plus that has become typical at US academic medical centers, where IIT regulatory and IRB burden has largely converged with industry-sponsored trials.]] in months rather than years, autologous manufacturing ran at a fraction of US cost, and US-trained cell-therapy scientists had been returning home through the Thousand Talents pipeline since the early 2010s. Domestic CAR-T manufacturing was already running, with [[entity:fosun-kite|Fosun Kite]] (a Shanghai JV with Kite/Gilead) producing Yescarta locally since summer 2021.[[cite:kite-fosun-first-cart-china]]

What worked in cell therapy generalized fast. Phase 3 oncology trials in China run roughly $25,000 per patient against $69,000 in the United States.[[cite:globaldata-oct-west-coast-2025]] For indications with deep treatment-naïve populations[[note:nasopharyngeal carcinoma, EGFR-mutant NSCLC, hepatitis-B-driven hepatocellular carcinoma, gastric cancer]], Chinese enrollment runs two to three times faster than US counterparts; over 40% of Chinese trials achieve high enrollment levels against US trials where most recent studies enroll fewer than 100 participants.[[cite:iqvia-rethinking-trial-prioritization-2024]] By 2024, China-headquartered companies were initiating 39% of all global oncology trials, up from roughly 5% a decade earlier.[[cite:iqvia-global-oncology-trends-2025]] CRDMO infrastructure ([[entity:wuxi-bio|WuXi Biologics]], [[entity:asymchem|Asymchem]], [[entity:pharmaron|Pharmaron]]) has become the physical substrate of global biopharma's small-molecule and biologics supply chain, and [[entity:bgi|BGI]]'s [[entity:mgi-tech|MGI Tech]] now undercuts [[entity:illumina|Illumina]] by roughly 2:1 on per-genome cost at ultra-high throughput.[[note:Illumina's NovaSeq X benchmarks at ~$200 per whole human genome at scale; MGI's DNBSEQ-T20x2 runs under $100/genome at very high volume.]]

title: The US Becomes Scared of CCP Spies
body:
[[entity:bgi|BGI]] was sitting on genomic data from millions of American patients, mostly collected through prenatal tests and sequencing-services contracts. China’s 2017 National Intelligence Law obligated any Chinese organization or citizen to “support, assist, and cooperate with” state intelligence work, which meant BGI could be compelled to hand that data over on demand; the company also had a documented history of co-authoring research with the People’s Liberation Army. [[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi]] was sitting under roughly a quarter of US drug-manufacturing supply chains, a single-point-of-failure dependency for the American pharmaceutical industry. By September 2024, Washington's concerns over Chinese bioweapons hit a tipping point, and the [[reform:biosecure|BIOSECURE Act]] passed the [[entity:us-congress|United States House of Representatives]] 306 to 81.[[cite:ropesgray-biosecure-house-2024]][[cite:mwc-biosecure-house-2024]] The Act restricted American executive-agency procurement and federal grants from “biotechnology companies of concern,” specifically capturing BGI, MGI, and WuXi.[[cite:foley-biosecure-2024]] In practice, any biotech receiving NIH funding, and any drug being purchased through Medicare or the Department of Defense, could no longer contract with these Chinese platforms.

In response, [[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi]] divested its American medical-device testing operations and its Oxford Genetics and WuXi Advanced Therapies cell-and-gene-therapy units.[[cite:wuxi-namsa-2025]][[cite:wuxi-altaris-atu-2024]][[cite:yicai-wuxi-altaris-2024]] [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] rebranded as [[entity:beigene|BeOne Medicines]] and redomiciled from the Cayman Islands to Basel, picking Switzerland for its political neutrality between the American and Chinese.[[cite:beone-launch-bw-2025]][[cite:beone-wikipedia]] American venture firms had largely withdrawn from China deals by 2025.[[cite:stat-china-biotech-foreign-pullback-2024]]

title: The AI-Bio Stack
body:
In 2025, [[entity:insilico|Insilico Medicine]] published positive Phase II data for [[entity:insilico|rentosertib]], a treatment for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis whose target, molecule, and trial design were all chosen and built by AI rather than by human researchers, with the target-discovery-to-PCC[[note:Preclinical Candidate Compound, the molecule selected as the lead for IND-enabling toxicology studies. The target-discovery-to-PCC cycle is the platform-defining metric for AI-discovery companies.]] cycle running roughly eighteen months at ~$2.6M. It was the first AI-discovered drug to clear a Phase II efficacy bar in humans.[[cite:insilico-rentosertib-press-2025]][[cite:nature-medicine-tnik-rentosertib-2025]] Insilico IPOed on the HKEX in December 2025 at a $2.28 billion valuation with a $290 million raise; the pipeline now spans thirty programs, eight at clinical stage.

[[entity:xtalpi|XtalPi]], founded by three MIT physicists in 2014, built its business on protein prediction. Originally focused solely on quantum-mechanics-based crystal structure prediction[[note: calculating which polymorph of a small molecule will form]], the platform has since expanded to combine quantum mechanical simulations with ML-based property prediction, as well as a heavily robotic wet lab in Shenzhen. The 2024 Hong Kong listing was the test case for the new Chapter 18C track, the specialist-technology rule designed to admit pre-revenue tech companies the way [[reform:hk-18a|Chapter 18A]] had for biotechs.[[cite:xtalpi-18c-listing-2024]] The 2025 research collaboration with DoveTree (founded by Harvard chemist Gregory Verdine, behind Warp Drive Bio and WaVe Life Sciences) was reportedly worth up to $10 billion.[[cite:xtalpi-dovetree-final-2025]][[cite:xtalpi-dovetree-loi-2025]]

Underneath the public-market AI-bio platforms sits a broader foundation-model layer[[note:Baidu's research arm has built HelixFold, an AlphaFold-competitive structure-prediction system; Tencent AI Lab runs a DrugFlow platform and pharma collaborations]]. [[entity:biomap|BioMap]], co-founded in late 2020 by Baidu CEO Robin Li, published xTrimoPGLM[[note:The architectural bet was that protein understanding (structure, function, fitness) and protein generation (de novo design) could be unified under a single hybrid GLM/MLM training objective rather than split between encoder-only models like ESM-2 and decoder-only models like ProtGPT2.]] in Nature Methods in April 2025, a 100-billion-parameter protein language model trained on 96 NVIDIA DGX-A100 nodes between January and June 2023. At 100B parameters, it is the largest protein language model with a public weights release.

Notably, Chinese AI-bio efforts largely run inside or adjacent to tech giants (Baidu, Tencent, Huawei) rather than as independent biotech-software companies. This is in stark contrast to the Western pattern, with Recursion, Insitro, Schrödinger, and Genesis operating as standalone entities connected to hyperscalers only through compute partnerships. Combined with [[entity:bgi|BGI]]/[[entity:mgi-tech|MGI]]’s sequencing throughput[[note:Stereo-seq matches 10x Genomics’ Visium HD on per-area list price (~€45/mm²) but delivers 16–80× more capture points at sub-cellular resolution (~220–500nm DNBs vs Visium HD’s 2µm bins).]] and the Chinese clinical-trial cycle-time advantage (Phase I enrollment runs 6–12 months from IND[[note:IND = Investigational New Drug application, the regulatory greenlight to begin human trials.]] in China versus 12–24 in the United States, with rentosertib clearing PCC[[note: PCC = preclinical candidate, the moment a project graduates from discovery to development.]]-to-first-dose in roughly 9 months), the result is a vertically integrated stack from data generation through clinical readout.

title: Patent Cliff and the Licensing Flywheel
shift: Western pharma's $200–230 billion patent cliff between 2025 and 2030 forces [[entity:merck-co|Merck]], [[entity:roche|Roche]], [[entity:pfizer|Pfizer]], and other major MNCs to acquire novel clinical-stage assets from China at unprecedented upfront values to fill the innovation gap.
outcomes: Average upfront payment on Western-Chinese licensing deals rose 230% between 2022 and early 2026, from $52M to $172M. [[metric:outLicensingDealValueBn|out-licensing $]]

[[entity:aiolos|Aiolos Bio]], built around [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]]'s anti-TSLP asthma antibody, was acquired by [[entity:gsk|GSK]] for up to $1.4B within 13 months of founding, establishing the NewCo template that re-routes Chinese assets through US incorporation to bypass [[reform:biosecure|BIOSECURE]].
Kailera Therapeutics raised $400M Series A (2024) around [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]]'s GLP-1 obesity portfolio, replicating the NewCo structure.
ADC drugs dominated H1 2025 Chinese license-outs, with 8 of 24 $1B+ deals featuring ADCs. [[metric:adc:4|adc rung 4]]

inlineCharts.title: Average upfront $ per Western-Chinese deal, by modality
inlineCharts.caption: Approximate medians, USD millions. ADCs lead the wave; bispecifics inflected after Akeso's HARMONi-2 readout in late 2024. Aggregate across all modalities rose ~230% from $52M (2022) to $172M (early 2026).

body:
[[chart:upfront-by-modality]]

[[entity:merck-co|Merck]]'s Keytruda alone generated $29.5 billion in 2024, more than half the company's revenue, with key patents expiring in 2028.[[note:Average new drug costs ~$2.23B to develop. Lifetime revenue runs ~5-8× peak annual sales, so a typical $510M-peak launch generates ~$2.5-4B over its patent life.]] The most active acquirers of Chinese assets by deal count are Bristol Myers Squibb, [[entity:roche|Roche]], and Merck; [[entity:pfizer|Pfizer]] and Gilead lead by dollar value. The Hang Seng Biotech Index ran roughly 60% in 2025 on the back of it (S&P 500 ran ~25%, US Nasdaq Biotech was roughly flat).

[[entity:beigene|BeOne]]'s relocation to Basel and [[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi]]'s American divestitures showed Chinese firms could maneuver around BIOSECURE exposure, but they were defensive moves. The NewCo structure is the latest innovation against retreating Western capital and [[reform:biosecure|BIOSECURE]]. Rather than investing in Chinese rounds directly, American VCs now fund new American biotechs that in-license assets from Chinese pharma. The Chinese parent keeps home-market rights and takes an upfront payment, milestones, and sometimes minority equity. The new biotech, US-incorporated and US-managed, runs Western development and is built for acquisition by Big Pharma; from a regulatory standpoint, the asset becomes an American product. [[entity:aiolos|Aiolos Bio]] was the first, founded in late 2023 by Atlas Venture and Forbion around an anti-TSLP asthma antibody licensed from [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]]. Thirteen months later, [[entity:gsk|GSK]] paid up to $1.4 billion to acquire it.[[cite:gsk-aiolos-press-2024]] Kailera Therapeutics followed in 2024 around Hengrui's GLP-1 obesity portfolio with a $400 million Series A from American venture firms.

The modalities where China leads largely reward iteration cycles over first-principles discovery, validated targets over novel mechanism, fast clinical readout over deep substrate science. [[entity:recursion|Recursion]]'s high-content-screening[[note:HCS combines automated microscopy with computational image analysis at industrial scale, generating phenotypic profiles for millions of perturbed cells across many cell types. It is the core data substrate for phenomics-trained foundation models.]] platform has no Chinese counterpart. The first-in-class therapeutic mechanisms driving recent Western pharma growth (GLP-1 receptor agonists, CFTR modulators, CRISPR gene-editing therapies) all originated in Western academic-and-industry partnerships, not Chinese ones.
